coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble carbonized polymer dots with tunable solid- and dispersion-state fluorescence for multicolor films, anti-counterfeiting, and fungal imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, C.
Shi, Q.
Zhang, Y.
Xie, X.
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with both superior solid/aggregation-state fluorescence (SSF) and dispersion-state fluorescence (DSF) are highly desired for biological, optoelectronic, and information safety applications due to their quenching-resistance and low toxicity. In this study, three matrix-free silicon-, europium/silicon-, and terbium/silicon-doped CPDs (Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs) possessing favorable powder white SSF and aqueous concentration-dependent DSF were achieved by introducing the unique structures of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores with abundant surface N, O, Si-related functional groups and polymer chains through one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The participations of Eu3+ and Tb3+ salts in the synthesis bestowed the corresponding Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs with higher graphitic N, accompanied by the red-shifted SSF and DSF emissions. Nevertheless, compared with Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs with ultratrace Eu (<0.001%) showed stronger powder photoluminescence quantum yield but weaker solution ones, while Tb/Si-CPDs with 0.96% Tb had both weaker powder and solution ones, suggesting the occurrence of electron transfer from CPDs to Eu3+ in dispersion state. The three CPDs solutions exhibited red-shifted luminescence from the cooperative carbon core and sub-fluorophores to the surfaces with their concentrations ranging from 1 to 200 mg/mL, causing the turning on of pale-yellow ice SSF. Interestingly, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films, formed from their solutions, presented unprecedented multicolor SSF from blue to red, initiated by CPDs skeletons and trace Eu3+/Tb3+, through modulating the excitation wavelength. Importantly, these advanced merits successfully expanded the applications of as-prepared CPDs in anti-counterfeiting and pathogenic fungi detection, affording a new perspective for designing versatile quenching-resistant optical materials.

Excitation-emission contour plots (d – f), and PL decay curves (g – i) of (d), (g) Si-CPDs, (e), (h) Eu/Si-CPDs, and (f), (i) Tb/Si-CPDs powders. CPD, carbonized polymer dot; PL, photoluminescence; UV–vis, ultraviolet–visible.

(a) XRD, (b) FTIR, and (c) full XPS spectra of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs. XPS high-resolution C 1s spectra of (d) Si-CPDs, (e) Eu/Si-CPDs, and (f) Tb/Si-CPDs. XPS high-resolution N 1s spectra of (g) Si-CPDs, (h) Eu/Si-CPDs, and (i) Tb/Si-CPDs. XPS high-resolution Si 2p spectra of (j) Si-CPDs, (k) Eu/Si-CPDs, and (l) Tb/Si-CPDs. CPD, carbonized polymer dot; FTIR, Fourier transform infrared; XPS, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy; XRD, X-ray diffraction.

The PL intensity of 1 mg/mL was set as control to compare the solution emission intensities of various concentrations; insets are the photos of different concentrations of the CPDs solutions under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. head.ce:title: Water-soluble carbonized polymer dots with tunable solid- and dispersion-state fluorescence for multicolor films, anti-counterfeiting, and fungal imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with both superior solid/aggregation-state fluorescence (SSF) and dispersion-state fluorescence (DSF) are highly desired for biological, optoelectronic, and information safety applications due to their quenching-resistance and low toxicity. In this study, three matrix-free silicon-, europium/silicon-, and terbium/silicon-doped CPDs (Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs) possessing favorable powder white SSF and aqueous concentration-dependent DSF were achieved by introducing the unique structures of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores with abundant surface N, O, Si-related functional groups and polymer chains through one-step hydrothermal synthesis. The participations of Eu3+ and Tb3+ salts in the synthesis bestowed the corresponding Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs with higher graphitic N, accompanied by the red-shifted SSF and DSF emissions. Nevertheless, compared with Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs with ultratrace Eu (<0.001%) showed stronger powder photoluminescence quantum yield but weaker solution ones, while Tb/Si-CPDs with 0.96% Tb had both weaker powder and solution ones, suggesting the occurrence of electron transfer from CPDs to Eu3+ in dispersion state. The three CPDs solutions exhibited red-shifted luminescence from the cooperative carbon core and sub-fluorophores to the surfaces with their concentrations ranging from 1 to 200 mg/mL, causing the turning on of pale-yellow ice SSF. Interestingly, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films, formed from their solutions, presented unprecedented multicolor SSF from blue to red, initiated by CPDs skeletons and trace Eu3+/Tb3+, through modulating the excitation wavelength. Importantly, these advanced merits successfully expanded the applications of as-prepared CPDs in anti-counterfeiting and pathogenic fungi detection, affording a new perspective for designing versatile quenching-resistant optical materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the serendipitous discovery by Xu et al. in 2004, nanometer-sized carbon dots (CDs) have been widely recognized as the great prospective luminescence carbon-based nanomaterials due to their advanced merits of tunable photoluminescence (PL), high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, feasibility of element doping and surface functionalization, and rich synthesis precursors and routes, and so on [1,2]. These brilliant features promote the development of CDs in various applications, such as optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, sensing and imaging, drug-delivery, anti-counterfeiting, and so forth [3–6]. To date, great improvement has been made for the development of high-performance CDs with the luminescence from blue to near-infrared region to fulfill different purposes [7–9]. Unfortunately, most reported CDs always suffer from self-quenching in their high-concentration solutions or solid/aggregation states because of the direct π–π stacking or excessive resonance energy transfer (RET), making their practical applications lag behind seriously. The common approaches for overcoming this aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect usually contain confining the CDs in matrices (such as mesoporous silica [10], epoxy resins [11], and Zeolite [12]) or functionalizing CDs' surface with polymers [13]. However, undesirable luminescence still happens because of the limited doping content of CDs in matrices, and the complexity and preparation cost also increase. Therefore, the development of novel matrix-free CDs with efficient dispersion-state fluorescence (DSF) and solid-state fluorescence (SSF) for expanding their applications in various fields is urgently desired.

Benefitted from the aggregation-induced emission mechanism that can suppress the π–π interaction or RET, there have been a number of reports about solid/aggregation-state luminescence CDs [14,15]. Most of these research studies are on hydrophobic CDs, ranging from the effect of solvents on their aggregation-state luminescence to the optical properties in solid state [16–21]. In contrast, water-soluble CDs, more advantageous for the biosensing application, simultaneously having DSF and SSF are rarely reported. As a subclass of CDs, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) offer a hopeful tactic for obtaining such multimode fluorescence and physicochemical properties due to their unique carbonized core covered with rich polymer chains [22,23]. Generally, the polymer chains can serve as matrices, stabilizing the corresponding luminescence centers of dispersion or solid/aggregation states, and their polarity can be regulated by the precursor attributes and synthesis methods [24]. Shao et al. synthesized quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescent CPDs, from maleic acid and ethylenediamine, with a concentration-controlled emission in water and matrix for realizing fluorescence ink and light-emitting diode applications [25]. By hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diethylenetriamine, Kwak et al. obtained a similar fluorescence behavior in solid state as that in dilute solution state [26]. Despite the progress achieved, the feasibility of these CPDs in biosensing and concentrated-solution luminescence has not been substantiated. Meanwhile, they still underwent the reduction of PL quantum yields (PLQYs) converting from solution to solid due to the RET and even required matrices to assist their fluorescence in solid/aggregation-state applications.

It has been demonstrated that heteroatom doping (e.g. N, S, Si, and rare earth [RE] ions, etc) has a significant impact on improving the luminescence properties and exploiting the potential applications of CDs [27–30]. Furthermore, the RE ions like europium (Eu3+) and terbium (Tb3+) can enrich the fluorescence modes of CDs-based optical materials by virtue of the distinct luminescence performance caused by their abundant 4f electron configurations [31]. However, the previous RE ion-doped CDs always show the luminescence from RE ions at the sacrifice of that of CDs due to the intense electron/energy transfer from CDs to RE ions [32,33], hampering their multimode fluorescence applications. Hence, it is of great application value to develop a new kind of matrix-free RE ion-doped CPDs with quenching-resistant multicolor luminescence from the synergistic effect of RE ions and CPDs.

In this work, we proposed a one-step hydrothermal strategy to prepare three heteroatom-doped CPDs with tunable SSF and concentration-controlled DSF. These obtained CPDs are composed of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores and hydrophilic N, O, Si-containing functional groups and polymer chains, contributing to the restriction of ACQ effect in concentrated solutions and solid/aggregation state. Contrasting with Si-doped CPDs (Si-CPDs), Eu/Si-doped CPDs (Eu/Si-CPDs) and Tb/Si-doped CPDs (Tb/Si-CPDs) with larger graphitic N contents showed red-shifted SSF and DSF emissions, but their DSF decreased, indicating the strong electron transfer between CPDs and trace Eu3+/Tb3+(<0.001%; 0.96%) in solutions. Nevertheless, Eu/Si-CPDs had stronger powder white SSF (PLQY: 14.1%), which could be beneficial to the SSF applications. The blue DSF of the three CPDs solutions became brighter when their concentrations increased (1–200 mg/mL), and the ice SSF turned on in high concentrations. Converted from aqueous solutions, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films exhibited tunable multicolor SSF from blue to red by changing the excitation source, which was induced by the cooperative effect of CPDs, Eu3+/Tb3+, and residual water molecules. On the basis of these superior characteristics, the as-prepared CPDs were successfully employed as multicolor luminescence inks and nanoprobes for anti-counterfeiting and pathogenic fungi detection applications.

l -Histidine (His, 98.5–101.5%) was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99%) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (99.9%) and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (99%) were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biochem Technology Co., Ltd., and dialysis bags (cellulose, 500–1000 Da) were provided by Shanghai Yuanye Bio-technology. The Milli-Q water (18.2 Ω, Millipore) was used to prepared aqueous solutions in this study. All chemical reagents were used directly.

The three heteroatom-doped CPDs were prepared by a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method at 160 °C. Si-CPDs: His (0.31 g) was dissolved in 30 mL water, and then 3 mL APTES was slowly added into the solution under stirring. The obtained mixture sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-line autoclave was placed in a microwave synthesizer and heated at 160 °C for 4 h, followed by cooling down to room temperature. Subsequently, the raw product was purified with a dialysis bag in Milli-Q water for 24 h and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the large particles. The purified solution was freeze-dried to get the Si-CPDs powder. Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs were also prepared in the same procedure, where Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (31 mg) and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (36 mg) were mixed with His in water before adding APTES, respectively.

The PL excitation, PL emission, and PL decay spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer. The PLQYs were measured by an Edinburgh FS1000 spectrofluorometer. A Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer and a Multiskan GO microplate reader were used to detect the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of solid and solution samples, respectively. A Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at a scan rate of 5°/min was carried out to collect the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images were collected on a JEOL JEM-2100 TEM using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were detected using a Brucker VERTEX 70 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were acquired by a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250XI spectrometer. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were tested by a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer. High-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were measured with a Thermo Scientific Q Exactive Combine quadrupole Orbitrap Mass spectrometer. Fluorescence imaging experiments of fungi were implemented with a Nikon Y-TV55 microscope.

The fungi of Candida albicans (C. albicans, ATCC 10231), Candida parapsilosis (C. parapsilosis, ATCC 22019), and Candida tropicalis (C. tropicalis, ATCC 20962) species used in this work were cultured in sabouraud dextrose broth in a dark shaking incubator (180 rpm, 37 °C). After 3 days, fresh fungi were collected by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 3 min) and washed with sterile phosphate buffered solution (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4). Before fluorescence imaging, three live Candida species were incubated with Eu/Si-CPDs solution (in PBS, 5 mg/mL) for 15, 30, 60 min, which were centrifuged and washed with fresh PBS three times. The treated fungi were placed on glass slides and then observed under the 60× oil-immersion objectives of the fluorescence microscope. Besides, the three dead fungi (acquired by treating at 100 °C for 30 min in a thermomixer) were also treated with Eu/Si-CPDs to evaluate the identification ability of Eu/Si-CPDs toward live and dead cells.

As depicted in Scheme 1 , the water-soluble Si-CPDs were hydrothermally synthesized at 160 °C using His as carbon source and APTES as silicon source; while silicon/europium-doped CPDs (Si/Eu-CPDs) and silicon/terbium-doped CPDs (Si/Tb-CPDs) were achieved by introducing additional Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O as RE sources in the same synthesis process, respectively. Interestingly, the three matrix-free mud-yellow (sunlight) powders of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs emitted white fluorescence under 365 nm excitation, but weak-white, reddish white, and yellow–green fluorescence under 254 nm excitation, respectively (Fig. 1 a–c, insets), indicating the tunable SSF. This phenomenon made them different from the most reported water-soluble CDs that require auxiliary matrices to show SSF. In order to get insight into the optical properties of the three powder samples, UV–vis absorption, PL excitation, and PL emission spectra were measured (Fig. 1). As can be seen, there was a small peak centered at around 210 nm and a broad absorption band across UV and visible-light regions (225–800 nm) for the UV–vis absorption spectra of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs (Fig. 1a–c, gray region), indicative of the formation of sp2-conjugated domains in the three samples. Notably, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs had distinct maximum excitation wavelengths centered, respectively, at around 415, 440, and 455 nm, accompanied by the correspondingly optimum emission wavelengths located at 500, 525, and 540 nm, suggesting the different surface state transitions. The obvious overlap between the PL emission and absorption spectra signified the strong reabsorption for the three powders [34]. The absolute PLQYs, common quantitative values for estimating the luminescence of CDs, were measured to be about 10.97%, 14.1%, and 9.1% for Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs (Fig. S1), respectively.

As illustrated by the excitation-emission contour plots in Fig. 1d–f, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs powders showed excitation-dependent characteristics covering a broad excitation wavelength range of 300–500 nm, indicating the multiemission sites for their solid-state powders. Besides, both the excitations and emissions of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs are red-shifted relative to Si-CPDs. Two additional narrow emissions were present at 610 and 615 nm for Eu/Si-CPDs upon the excitation at 300–400 nm, while four new narrow ones emerged at 490, 545, 585, and 620 nm for Tb/Si-CPDs under the excitation of 300–350 nm, ascribed to the classical luminescence of Eu3+ (5D0→7F2) and Tb3+ (5D4→7F6, 5D4→7F5, 5D4→7F4, and 5D4→7F3) [35], respectively. These findings hinted that the luminescence properties of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs had integrated those of Eu3+/Tb3+ and origin CPDs. Additionally, the time-resolved PL decay spectra indicated that the three powders had analogous photophysical properties (Fig. 1g–i; Table S1). Si-CPDs powder displayed a tri-exponential function decay profile upon 405 nm excitation, with an average lifetime estimated as 5.43 ns. Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs powders also manifested a tri-exponential function, but their average lifetimes slightly decreased to 5.26 and 5.02 ns, respectively. The multiexponential fitted lifetimes implied that the three powder samples went through multiple decay channels during the PL decay processes [17,35].

In order to understand the powder PL origin of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs, detailed structural characterizations were tested. In the XRD patterns (Fig. 2 a), a clear broad peak originated from the graphite carbon (001) crystal plane appeared at about 22° [36], indicating the formation of graphitization structure for these CPDs. As observed in Fig. S2, their HRMS showed multiple peaks and the corresponding relative ion mass difference was matched with the integer multiples of the relative molecular mass of H2O (18n), confirming that APTES and His went through dehydration, polycondensation, and carbonization in the formation of the three CPDs. As for the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b), similar absorption peaks were obtained for Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs, disclosing the homologous functional groups and chemical bonds assembled on their surfaces. The strong stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H polar functional groups occurred at around 3436 cm, and the bands located at 2936 and 2866 cm reflected to the C–H stretching vibrations. The vibration absorption signals at 1647, 1415–1385, and 1339 cm could be attributed to the C]O, C]C/C]N, and C–O groups, respectively, while the absorption bands at about 1581 and 1479 cm were ascribed to the in-plane bending vibrations of N–H and C–H, respectively, substantiating the presence of polyaromatic heterocyclic structures of the three CPDs. Moreover, the absorption peaks position at 1137 and 1027 cm belonged to the C–N and Si–O–Si stretching vibrations, respectively. These functional groups could endow the three CPDs with remarkable water solubility. In addition, 13C NMR spectra were used to reveal the hybridization states of the carbon atoms from these CPDs. As shown in Fig. S3, the multiple signals assigned to the non-conjugated hybridized C at 10–60 ppm were more intense for Si-CPDs, while the detected peaks of conjugated sp2-hybridized C at 118–135 ppm were more obvious for Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs, demonstrating the higher graphitization extent of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs. In addition, the signal at around 182 ppm was indicative of the C]N/C]O groups of the CPDs, in agreement with the FTIR result.

XPS was further applied to investigate the chemical structures of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs. The full survey XPS results in Fig. 2c showed that the three CPDs mainly consisted of C, N, O and Si elements, and the corresponding element contents (Table S2) were similar (C: 52.14–55.46%; N: 10.45–11.81%; O: 22.66–24.59%; and Si: 10.12–11.61%). Notably, the Eu content in Eu/Si-CPDs was much less than 0.001 wt% while the Tb content in Tb/Si-CPDs was determined to be about 0.96 wt% based on the inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry results, which accounted for the absence of their signals in the XPS spectra. The ultratrace Eu doping level was probably due to the difficult anchoring capability of Eu3+ to Eu/Si-CPDs. The XPS high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2d–f) of the three CPDs could be deconvoluted into four peaks situated at 283.78, 284.81, 286.25, and 288.33 eV, corresponding to the C–Si, C–C/C]C, C–N/C–O, and C]O, respectively. The N 1s bands (Fig. 2g–i) exhibited four peaks originated from the pyridinic N (398.29 eV), amino N (399.03 eV), pyrrolic N (399.93 eV), and graphitic N (401.03 eV), respectively. The O 1s spectra (Fig. S4) suggested that the O element existed in the patterns of O]C (530.47 eV), O–Si (532.03 eV), and O–C (533.29 eV) in the three CPDs. For the Si 2p spectra (Fig. 2j–l), Si element was in two environments including Si–O–Si/C (102.45 eV) and Si–O–H (101.67 eV), further proving the successful introduction of Si element in Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs.

As illustrated in Tables S3–4, the quantitative data of different groups obtained by XPS analysis showed that the C–C/C]C content for Si-CPDs (60.51%) was lower than that for Eu/Si-CPDs (71.59%) and Tb/Si-CPDs (73.25%), revealing the larger sp2-hybridized carbon cores of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs, just as demonstrated by 13C NMR spectra. Comparatively, the C–N/C–O content in Si-CPDs (30.16%) was over twofold with respect to that in Eu/Si-CPDs (14.36%) and Tb/Si-CPDs (14.38%), in accordance with the variation trend of C–O proportion in O 1s spectra, whereas the C]O levels of Si-CPDs were minimal. It illuminates that Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs possessed higher oxidation degree with the treatment of Eu3+ and Tb3+ salts, respectively. It has been reported that graphitic N in the sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon core can donate its lone-pair electrons to increase the entire π electron cloud density and generate mid-gap states between the highest occupied molecular orbital and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital gap, hence its increase can reduce the non-radiative recombination and narrow the energy level gap, which is favorable for improving the optical properties of CPDs [14,37–39]. As listed in Table S3, Eu/Si-CPDs (31.16%) and Tb/Si-CPDs (26.87%) had larger graphitic N content than Si-CPDs (19.51%), leading to their red-shifted PL excitation/emission. Besides, the increased pyrrolic N with electron-donating nature can also contribute to the electron cloud density of sp2-conjugated system, conducive to the enhancement of PLQYs [40]. However, unlike Eu/Si-CPDs, Tb/Si-CPDs obtained lower powder PLQY (Fig. S1) despite their higher graphitic and pyrrolic N contents in comparison with Si-CPDs. It was probably due to the electron transfer from the surface to Tb3+ under photo-induced excitation [31], which may also be the cause for the stronger emissions of Tb3+ than that of origin CPDs (Fig. 1f). Moreover, the surface Si–O–Si/C and Si–O–H-related groups and polymer chains proved by XPS (Table S4) and FTIR analyses could form the ‘steric hindrance’ network between adjacent CPDs to restrain the ACQ induced by the excess RET or direct π–π stacking [41], thereby maintaining the powder SSF of these CPDs. Overall, the consistent results of XRD, HRMS, FTIR, and 13C NMR indicated that the sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores of the three CPDs were functionalized with N, O, Si-related functional groups/polymer chains, producing the fascinating tunable solid-state luminescence. The red-shifted luminescence happened when the graphitic N content increased, while the Eu and Tb play an opposite effect on the powder PLQYs due to their doping content difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of aqueous CPDs solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the rich surface hydrophilic groups, involving –COOH, –OH, –NH2, and –SiOH, of the three CPDs, their possible optical characteristics in aqueous dispersion state (water) were also explored. As seen in Fig. 3 a–c, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs solutions exhibited tunable DSF, which changed from faint blue to white–blue (under 365 nm excitation) when the concentration increased from 1 to 200 mg/mL, demonstrative of the concentration-dependent luminescence. Interestingly, the three aqueous samples displayed different emission behaviors with the increased concentration (Fig. S5). The excitation-emission contour plots of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs solutions showed that the main emissions of 1 and 5 mg/mL were almost unchanged, indicating the excitation-independent emission property. It suggests that there was only one emission site for the three CPDs at these dilute concentrations. Whereas, the concentrated solutions of 20, 50, and 200 mg/mL aroused more emission sites, leading to the excitation-dependent feature. Nonetheless, the red-shifted DSF emissions of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs solutions were more remarkable with the incremental concentrations (Fig. 3a–c), along with the disappearing emissions of Eu3+ and Tb3+. Additionally, the DSF intensities of the three CPDs solutions showed parabolic changes in the concentration range of 1–200 mg/mL, of which Si-CPDs achieved a maximum at 50 mg/mL, while Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs acquired the maxima at 20 mg/mL. However, the solution PLQYs of the three CPDs were negative to their concentrations (Fig. S6), and their respective highest value of 8.13%, 6.97%, and 6.9% appeared at 1 mg/mL, which were weaker to the powder ones. Besides, the solution PLQYs of both Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs were inferior to that of Si-CPDs at the same concentration. Upon the same excitation, Si-CPDs exhibited the strongest PL intensity among the three samples at the concentration of 1, 5, 20, 50, and 200 mg/mL, respectively (Fig. S7), suggesting the electron transfer from CPDs to Eu3+ and Tb3+ in solutions. When these CPDs solutions were frozen into ice, their fluorescence intensities were positive to the concentration (Fig. S8b and d–f). It is noted that the ice-state fluorescence was greatly weakened in comparison with the corresponding DSF in the concentration of 1–20 mg/mL, which was similar to the molecular state fluorescence of aqueous RhB samples (Fig. S8a and c), indicating the presence of sub-fluorophores on the surface of the three CPDs [42]. This phenomenon was gradually inverted at higher concentration, suggesting the inactive sub-fluorophores. These results demonstrated that the DSF and ice SSF of the three CPDs were tunable by adjusting the concentration, which could be advantageous for the practical quenching-resistant applications.

In Fig. S9, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs solutions exhibited similar UV–vis absorption spectra at the same concentrations, and the absorbance before 500 nm rose significantly and then declined gradually upon increasing the concentration. The absorption band at ∼280 nm attributed to the π–π∗ transitions of C]C and C]N in the conjugated carbon cores of the three CPDs, implying that the carbon core contributed to the DSF of dilute solutions (1–5 mg/mL). The red-shifted adsorption peaks from ∼305 to ≥415 nm in range of 20–200 mg/mL indicated that the DSF caused by the n-π∗ transition of surface C]N/C]O/C–N/C–O/Si–O became dominant. It is important to note that the photon reabsorption of the CPDs solutions enhanced progressively when raising the concentration, thus resulting in the decrease of PLQYs. These absorption spectra were then transformed into Tauc plots to calculate the optical band gaps of different concentrations of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs solutions. As shown in Fig. S10 and Table S5, the band gap energies of the three samples reduced gradually while elevating the concentrations, attributed to the quantum confinement effect [43]. On another hand, the band gap energy of Si-CPDs is slightly over that of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs at the same concentration, further indicating the availability of electron transfer from CPDs to Eu3+/Tb3+. Furthermore, the time-resolved PL decay curves were collected to explore the PL dynamic processes of different concentrations of the three CPDs solutions. As illustrated by Fig. 3d–f and the corresponding fitting kinetic parameters in Table S6, the PL decay curves revealed similar multichannel decay processes as that of their solid-state powders. But the average solution PL lifetimes were shorter than the powder ones, which was presumably because of the stronger movement of carriers or higher energy dissipation [44]. With an increase in concentration, the average solution lifetime of Si-CPDs regularly decreased from 5.02 to 3.93 ns, revealing the formation of new recombination channels in concentrated solutions [45,46]. Whereas, the average solution lifetimes of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs showed irregular changes against the concentrations, which was possibly associated with the interaction between Eu3+/Tb3+ and carbon cores or surface groups/polymer chains.

To further explain the effect of CPDs concentration on their optical properties, the microscopic morphologies of 1, 5, and 20 mg/mL were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Si-CPDs were dispersed with an average diameter of 1.84 ± 0.78 nm in 1 mg/mL solution. A well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm could be observed in the high-resolution TEM (inset) image, assigned to the (100) facets of graphitic carbon core. At the concentration of 5 mg/mL, the TEM image of Si-CPDs presented more quasi-spherical particles with an average diameter of 1.43 ± 0.32 nm. Further, about twice bigger average diameter of 3.24 ± 0.66 nm was detected at the concentration of 20 mg/mL due to the discrete aggregate of Si-CPDs; several great larger aggregates in random shapes were also observed. These results indicated that Si-CPDs were able to agglomerate into dispersive aggregates of different extent with the variation of their concentration, which could inhibit the rapid attenuation of PLQYs because of their self-protection structure. As for Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs, they were uniformly dispersed in 1 mg/mL solutions (Fig. 4b and c), exhibiting similar average diameters of around 1.21 ± 0.24 nm with identical lattice fringe spacings of 0.21 nm. Subsequently, increasingly dense particles of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs with uniform appearance were found upon increasing the concentrations. Distinctly, Tb/Si-CPDs were severely adhesive and formed into consecutive aggregate structure at the concentration of 20 mg/mL, leading to the difficulty in measuring their diameter. These morphologies suggested that the homogeneous distribution of CPDs in water could be more prone to intensify the vibration and rotation of their surface hydrophilic groups by collision or H-bond/static interactions, even worse in high concentration, which could strengthen the non-radiative transition [14,47] and in turn caused the significant reduction of solution PLQYs. Interestingly, the three CPDs failed to disperse stably in the common polar organic solvents, such as ethanol. As depicted in Fig. 4d–f, the transparent CPDs solutions became turbid with the increase of ethanol volume content and nearly precipitated at 90% ethanol. This characteristic made the PL intensities of Tb/Si-CPDs samples regularly improved, while led to a small reduction one for both Si-CPDs and Eu/Si-CPDs systems containing 90% ethanol. It reveals that the aggregated CPDs induced by poor solvent can enhance their fluorescence through suppressing the energy depletion or non-radiative relaxation but do not modulate their emissive states, which distinguishes from the reported aggregation-induced emission materials that can simultaneously change the luminescence centers and intensities [14,20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible PL mechanism of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The above results manifest that the three heteroatom-doped CPDs have been successfully prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Due to their unique structures composed of sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores and plenteous surface N, O, Si-related functional groups/polymer chains, Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs showed tunable quenching-resistant fluorescence in different states. The corresponding possible luminescence mechanisms were described in Fig. S11. For the dilute solutions (<20 mg/mL), most CPDs particles could move freely in water in spite of the remarkable H-bond interaction between the surface N, O-containing groups and water molecules, so that the cooperation of carbon core and sub-fluorophores could trigger the excitation-independent blue DSF under UV excitation. In this condition, the vibration and rotation of surface groups/polymer chains could promote the electron transfer to Eu3+ and Tb3+, resulting in the lower PLQYs of Eu/Si-CPDs and Tb/Si-CPDs dilute solutions (Fig. S6) [35]. In concentrated solutions, the collision probability between adjacent CPDs increased, and the movement of surface groups/polymer chains intensifies on account of their steric repulsion as well as the inter/intra-molecular H-bond competition, leading to the energy transition from carbon core to surfaces and the increase of non-radiative recombination. As a consequence, the DSF from carbon core and sub-fluorophores turned off, while that with longer wavelength from surface state turned on, leading to the tunable DSF. Besides, the increased non-radiative consumption and photon reabsorption could make the PLQYs fall off, and the change of radiative recombination centers was probably the reason for the attenuation of the average lifetimes. When the CPDs aggregated in poor solvent, the sluggish surface movement and vibration could impair the non-radiative depletion of carrier, thus achieving the stronger emissions [47]. For the solid-state powders, because of their heavily restrictive movement and vibration, the surface N, O, Si-related groups/polymers chains (such as Si–O–Si–C/N) could serve as a large steric net for keeping the adjacent particles in a safety distance, blocking the energy transfer process and further whittling non-radiative carrier dissipation. Hence, the SSF from carbon core and surfaces of the CPDs was sustained, as proved by the adsorption spectra across the UV (π–π∗ transition) and visible regions (n–π∗ transition), with the concomitant increases in lifetimes and PLQYs. Generally, the incremental graphitic N can increase the π–electron cloud density, which endowed Eu/Si-CPDs powder with higher PLQYs and red-shifted emission. However, it was not suitable for Tb/Si-CPDs with lower PLQY, which was possibly because the trace Tb content could induce more excited state electrons to transfer from surfaces/carbon core to Tb3+ [12], causing the strong emissions of Tb3+ under ≤310 nm excitation (Fig. 1F). These findings are different from the reported SSF CDs that show shorter lifetimes and weaker PLQYs in solid/aggregate states relative to their solutions [48–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multicolor fluorescence of CPDs films and safety inks
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding quenching-resistant fluorescence performances in solution and aggregation/solid states enabled the three CPDs to be processed to realize different applications. Fig. 5 a showed the yellow Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs solid films, which were prepared by naturally drying their aqueous solutions on a polyethylene glove without any matrix. Satisfactorily, the three porous film samples exhibited multicolor SSF with diverging light by tuning the excitation wavelength. As the excitation changed from 365 to 254 nm, the SSF of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films turned from bright nattier blue, white, and aqua colors to light–blue, red, and chartreuse colors, respectively, likely associated with their multiemissive features. As shown in Fig. S12, Si-CPDs film exhibited dual-emission behavior at about 580 and 730 nm with an excitation ranging from 300 to 500 nm; whereas Eu/Si-CPDs film showed three narrow emissions belonging to Eu3+ at around 580, 614, and 690 nm under 250–400 nm excitation, and then displayed a single broad emission, centered at 575 nm, attributed to the surface state with longer excitation. Tb/Si-CPDs film presented four narrow emissions from Tb3+ of 540, 580, 640, and 730 nm under 250–350 nm excitation, while broad dual-emissions of 525 and 735 nm by increasing the excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, both the emissions of Eu3+ and Tb3+ at 614 and 540 nm, respectively, were stronger than the other emissions, conducive to the red and chartreuse SSF. These phenomena demonstrated the truly combinative luminescence merits of both Eu3+/Tb3+ and CPDs, presumably due to the formation of new multiemissive centers, and the participation of surface groups/polymer chains and residual immobilized H2O molecules in the electron or energy transfer among multiemission centers. Furthermore, the PLQYs of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films were detected to be 7.23%, 7.5%, and 6.53%, respectively (Fig. S13a), approaching to those of their dilute solutions. The corresponding average lifetimes were calculated to be about 9.52, 6.83, 6.32 ns, respectively (Fig. S13b and Table S7), which were longer to those of their powders. Besides, the PL intensities of the three CPDs films maintained nearly constant under continuous UV light irradiation (365 nm, 24 h), confirming their superior photostability (Fig. S14a). Each CPDs film exhibits good transparency and flexibility (Fig. S14b). In another aspect, the three solutions were painted on a filter paper with pen-brushes and dried naturally (Fig. 5b and c). The names of Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs (second row) were invisible under sunlight, but the letters of ‘GIM’ (Fig. 5b) and the flower (Fig. 5c) were clear after multiple depictions. Under 365 nm excitation, these images exhibited the similar fluorescence as the films, which became more colorful under 254 nm excitation. The long lifetimes and attractive fluorescence demonstrate that the three hydrophilic CPDs have great prospects in optoelectronic devices (such as light-emitting diodes and displays) and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CPDs in staining Candida species
ce:section.ce:para: Candida species are one of the main causes of fungal infections in hospitalized patients, which can result in high mortalities [51,52]. Conventional detection methods of Candida species, such as polymerase-chain reactions, histopathology, and cell cultures, are time-consuming and tedious [52,53]. Thus, to develop a valid method for the rapid and precise detection of Candida species is highly needed. CDs-based fluorescence imaging has attracted considerable attentions in cell identification or intracellular detection due to its non-destructive, in-situ, and visual properties. [54,55], which would be powerful for detecting the Candida species. In this work, the prepared Eu/Si-CPDs with intriguing fluorescence feature, as a representative, were selected to investigate their potential staining performance for Candida species. Three prevalent pathogenic Candida species of Candida albicans (C. albicans), Candida parapsilosis (C. parapsilosis), and Candida tropicalis (C. tropicalis) were incubated with Eu/Si-CPDs under physiological environment at 37 °C, and their fluorescence signals were collected by a fluorescence microscope. As displayed in Fig. 6 , under identical culture conditions, the three Candida species exhibited distinct morphologies, of which C. albicans emerged in the forms of yeast, pseudohyphae, and hyphase, while C. parapsilosis existed in the shape of yeast, and C. tropicalis exhibited smaller yeast form. Upon incubating with Eu/Si-CPDs from 15 to 60 min, only a few cells of the live C. tropicalis showed bright blue fluorescence signals, indicating longer staining time (30–60 min) could not improve this phenomenon (Fig. 6a). As for the case of live C. albicans and C. tropicalis, with the incubation time of 60 min, strong fluorescence signals were observed in the pseudohyphae and hyphase cells of C. albicans, whereas only several cells of C. parapsilosis were successfully stained by Eu/Si-CPDs. This finding is different from the reported probes that can enter all/most the live or dead cells of the same species [55]. It was presumably due to the interaction between the special hydrophilic substance in the above stained cells and the Eu/Si-CPDs with abundant –NH2, –OH, and –COOH groups. Besides, it may be also associated with hydrophilic/hydrophobic surfaces of these Candida species [51,56]. The hydrophilic Eu/Si-CPDs were easier to interact with the comparatively higher hydrophilic surfaces of the stained cells of C. tropicalis, and then the appendiculate Eu/Si-CPDs diffused into these cells across cell wall and membrane, leading to the short internalization time. Instead, resulting from the repulsive interaction against less hydrophilic surfaces, Eu/Si-CPDs needed much longer time to enter the pseudohyphae and hyphase cells of C. albicans and the stained yeast cells of C. parapsilosis through the slow endocytosis effect. Furthermore, the Eu/Si-CPDs were also utilized for staining these dead Candida species (obtained via treating at 100 °C for 30 min). As shown in Fig. 6b (right column), Eu/Si-CPDs could be effectively internalized into the yeast cells of dead C. albicans, C. parapsilosis, and C. tropicalis but not the pseudohyphae and hyphase cells of dead C. albicans. Note that uneven distribution of fluorescence signals was found in the dead C. albicans and C. tropicalis cells, possibly due to the content difference of specific substance that Eu/Si-CPDs can interact with in various cells. In addition, among these dead cells, the fluorescence signals in C. parapsilosis cells were the brightest. This result suggested that the rapid penetration of Eu/Si-CPDs into most yeast cells of the three dead Candida species with damage surface was feasible by passive diffusion [55]. Therefore, Eu/Si-CPDs are available to facilely and effectively discriminate the three live and dead Candida species by the localization and intensity of fluorescence signal, as well as the stained time.

In summary, we have designed three heteroatom-doped CPDs with multicolor SSF and DSF by a facile one-step hydrothermal method and explored their luminescence properties and potential applications. Remarkably, the as-prepared Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs have the superior structures of abundant N, O, Si-related hydrophilic groups/polymer chains covering around the sp2/sp3-hybridized carbon cores, contributing to the excellent water solubility and quenching-resistant powder white fluorescence. With the increased graphitic N content, Eu/Si-CPDs with ultratrace Eu (<0.001%) presented stronger red-shifted emission in powder state but weaker emissions in solutions than Si-CPDs, while Tb/Si-CPDs with 0.96% Tb content showed both weaker SSF and DSF emissions, revealing the strong electron transfer from CPDs to Eu3+/Tb3+ in solutions. Besides, the three CPDs solutions displayed red-shifted DSF from faint blue to white–blue upon increasing the concentrations from 1 to 200 mg/mL, and the pale-yellow ice SSF became apparent. Dried from the solutions, the obtained Si-CPDs, Eu/Si-CPDs, and Tb/Si-CPDs films emitted unprecedented tunable multicolor SSF from blue to red under different excitations, owing to the synergistic luminescence of both CPDs and Eu3+/Tb3+. These novel structures and PL properties of the three CPDs have enabled their applications in multicolor anti-counterfeiting and distinguishing pathogenic Candida species. In brief, this study offers an opportunity of developing versatile water-soluble CPDs with tunable DSF and SSF, further advancing the extensive applications of photonic devices, inks, and probes, and so on.
